   761→                            isDefault={hue === DEFAULT_HUE}
   762→                          />
   763→                          <Popover.Arrow className="fill-[hsl(0_0%_10%)]" />
   764→                        </Popover.Content>
   765→                      </Popover.Portal>
   766→                    </Popover.Root>
   767→                  </div>
   768→
   769→                  {/* Lightness header — right column only */}
   770→                  <div className="col-start-2">
   771→                    <span className="text-[10px] font-mono text-[hsl(0_0%_60%)] mb-1 block">Lightness</span>
   772→                    <LightnessHeader />
   773→                  </div>
   774→
   775→                  {/* 0% sat */}
   776→                  <div>
   777→                    <div className="grid grid-cols-[1fr_auto] items-baseline gap-2 mb-1">
   778→                      <p className="text-[13px] font-semibold text-white/70">Achromatic Baseline</p>
   779→                      <span className="text-[10px] font-mono text-[hsl(0_0%_60%)]">Saturation: 0%</span>
   780→                    </div>
   781→                    <p className="text-[13px] leading-relaxed text-white/45">
   782→                      Unsaturated grayscale. Functional, but lacks character or brand identity.
   783→                    </p>
   784→                  </div>
   785→                  <Strip colors={strips.zero} />
   786→
   787→                  {/* 20% sat */}
   788→                  <div>
   789→                    <div className="grid grid-cols-[1fr_auto] items-baseline gap-2 mb-1">
   790→                      <p className="text-[13px] font-semibold text-white/70">Fixed low saturation</p>
   791→                      <span className="text-[10px] font-mono text-[hsl(0_0%_60%)]">Saturation: {Math.round(SAT_FOR_MID * 100)}%</span>
   792→                    </div>
   793→                    <p className="text-[13px] leading-relaxed text-white/45">
   794→                      Desired neutral midtones, but dark and light ends become effectively desaturated.
   795→                    </p>
   796→                  </div>
   797→                  <Strip colors={strips.forMid} mark={markForMid} />
   798→
   799→                  {/* 40% sat */}
   800→                  <div>
   801→                    <div className="grid grid-cols-[1fr_auto] items-baseline gap-2 mb-1">
   802→                      <p className="text-[13px] font-semibold text-white/70">Fixed high saturation</p>
   803→                      <span className="text-[10px] font-mono text-[hsl(0_0%_60%)]">Saturation: {Math.round(SAT_FOR_EXTREMES * 100)}%</span>
   804→                    </div>
   805→                    <p className="text-[13px] leading-relaxed text-white/45">
   806→                      Raising it to recover the extremes pushes the midrange
   807→                      to be oversaturated.
   808→                    </p>
   809→                  </div>
   810→                  <Strip colors={strips.forExtremes} mark={markForExtremes} />
   811→
   812→                  {/* The solution — spans both columns, uses its own 1fr 1fr subgrid */}
   813→                  <div className="col-span-2">
   814→                    <SolutionSection hue={hue} />
   815→                  </div>
   816→
   817→                  {/* Boundary strip */}
   818→                  <div>
   819→                    <div className="grid grid-cols-[1fr_auto] items-baseline gap-2 mb-1">
   820→                      <p className="text-[13px] font-semibold text-white/70">Perceptual boundary</p>
   821→                      <span className="text-[10px] font-mono text-[hsl(0_0%_60%)]">
   822→                        {"Saturation: "}
   823→                        {Math.round(BOUNDARY_SCALE * getSaturation(LIGHTNESS_LABELS[0], REF_SMOD, "endpoint") * 100)}
   824→                        {"/"}
   825→                        {Math.round(BOUNDARY_SCALE * getSaturation(LIGHTNESS_LABELS[4], REF_SMOD, "endpoint") * 100)}
   826→                        {"/"}
   827→                        {Math.round(BOUNDARY_SCALE * getSaturation(LIGHTNESS_LABELS[8], REF_SMOD, "endpoint") * 100)}%
   828→                      </span>
   829→                    </div>
   830→                    <p className="text-[13px] leading-relaxed text-white/45">
   831→                      Sampling saturation along the curve produces even
   832→                      perceived chroma, but is too saturated for neutrals.
   833→                    </p>
   834→                  </div>
   835→                  <Strip colors={strips.boundary} mark={markCurved} />
   836→
   837→                  {/* Final result strip */}
   838→                  <div>
   839→                    <div className="grid grid-cols-[1fr_auto] items-baseline gap-2 mb-1">
   840→                      <p className="text-[13px] font-semibold text-white/70">Adjusted curve</p>
   841→                      <span className="text-[10px] font-mono text-[hsl(0_0%_60%)]">
   842→                        {"Saturation: "}
   843→                        {Math.round(SAT_CURVED * getSaturation(LIGHTNESS_LABELS[0], REF_SMOD, "endpoint") * 100)}
   844→                        {"/"}
   845→                        {Math.round(SAT_CURVED * getSaturation(LIGHTNESS_LABELS[4], REF_SMOD, "endpoint") * 100)}
   846→                        {"/"}
   847→                        {Math.round(SAT_CURVED * getSaturation(LIGHTNESS_LABELS[8], REF_SMOD, "endpoint") * 100)}%
   848→                      </span>
   849→                    </div>
   850→                    <p className="text-[13px] leading-relaxed text-white/45">
   851→                      Reducing the base saturation scales the whole curve
   852→                      down. The formula preserves the ratio between
   853→                      midtones and extremes at any base value.
   854→                    </p>
   855→                  </div>
   856→                  <Strip colors={strips.curved} mark={markCurved} />
   857→                </div>
   858→
   859→                <div className="mt-8 pt-4 border-t border-white/[0.06] text-[11px] text-white/30 grid gap-1.5">
   860→                  <p>
   861→                    <sup className="text-[9px] text-white/25 mr-1">1</sup>
   862→                    Concept from{" "}
   863→                    <a
   864→                      href="https://medium.com/design-bootcamp/the-secret-to-creating-neutral-color-palettes-5e5a650b1718"
   865→                      target="_blank"
   866→                      rel="noopener noreferrer"
   867→                      className="text-white/50 hover:text-white/70 underline underline-offset-2 transition-colors"
   868→                    >
   869→                      The secret to creating neutral color palettes
   870→                    </a>
   871→                    {" "}by Pablo Figueiredo.
   872→                  </p>
   873→                  <p>
   874→                    <sup className="text-[9px] text-white/25 mr-1">2</sup>

   876→                    <a
   877→                      href="https://www.desmos.com/calculator/02ufrfsuzy"

   885→                  </p>
   886→                </div>

   902→                        fill="currentColor"

   906→                    </svg>

   911→          </Dialog.Portal>

     1→import chroma from "chroma-js";
     2→import type { ColorEntry, ExportFormat, ColorSpace } from "../types/palette";
     3→
     4→function colorValue(entry: ColorEntry, space: ColorSpace): string {
     5→  const c = chroma(entry.hex);
     6→  switch (space) {
     7→    case "hsl":
     8→      return `hsl(${entry.hslString})`;
     9→    case "hex":
    10→      return entry.hex;
    11→    case "rgb": {
    12→      const [r, g, b] = c.rgb();
    13→      return `rgb(${r}, ${g}, ${b})`;
    14→    }
    15→    case "hcl": {
    16→      const [h, c2, l] = c.hcl();
    17→      const hR = Math.round(h || 0);
    18→      const cR = Math.round((c2 || 0) * 10) / 10;
    19→      const lR = Math.round(l * 10) / 10;
    20→      return `hcl(${hR}, ${cR}, ${lR})`;
    21→    }
    22→    case "oklch": {
    23→      const [l, c2, h] = c.oklch();
    24→      const lR = Math.round(l * 1000) / 1000;
    25→      const cR = Math.round((c2 || 0) * 1000) / 1000;
    26→      const hR = Math.round(h || 0);
    27→      return `oklch(${lR} ${cR} ${hR})`;
    28→    }
    29→  }
    30→}
    31→
    32→export function formatPalette(
    33→  entries: ColorEntry[],
    34→  format: ExportFormat,
    35→  space: ColorSpace,
    36→): string {
    37→  switch (format) {
    38→    case "css":
    39→      return formatCSS(entries, space);
    40→    case "tw4":
    41→      return formatTailwindV4(entries, space);
    42→    case "tw3":
    43→      return formatTailwindV3(entries, space);
    44→    case "scss":
    45→      return formatSCSS(entries, space);
    46→    case "js":
    47→      return formatJS(entries, space);
    48→    case "json":
    49→      return formatJSON(entries, space);
    50→    case "gpl":
    51→      return formatGPL(entries);
    52→    case "text":
    53→      return formatText(entries, space);
    54→  }
    55→}
    56→
    57→function formatCSS(entries: ColorEntry[], space: ColorSpace): string {
    58→  const vars = entries
    59→    .map((e) => `  --${e.label}: ${colorValue(e, space)};`)
    60→    .join("\n");
    61→  return `:root {\n${vars}\n}`;
    62→}
    63→
    64→function formatTailwindV4(entries: ColorEntry[], space: ColorSpace): string {
    65→  const vars = entries
    66→    .map((e) => `  --color-${e.label}: ${colorValue(e, space)};`)
    67→    .join("\n");
    68→  return `@theme {\n${vars}\n}`;
    69→}
    70→
    71→function formatTailwindV3(entries: ColorEntry[], space: ColorSpace): string {
    72→  const lines: string[] = [];
    73→  for (const e of entries) {
    74→    lines.push(`        ${String(e.lightness)}: "${colorValue(e, space)}",`);
    75→  }
    76→  return [
    77→    "/** @type {import('tailwindcss').Config} */",
    78→    "module.exports = {",
    79→    "  theme: {",
    80→    "    extend: {",
    81→    "      colors: {",
    82→    '        grey: {',
    83→    ...lines,
    84→    "        },",
    85→    "      },",
    86→    "    },",
    87→    "  },",
    88→    "};",
    89→  ].join("\n");
    90→}
    91→
    92→function formatSCSS(entries: ColorEntry[], space: ColorSpace): string {
    93→  return entries
    94→    .map((e) => `$${e.label}: ${colorValue(e, space)};`)
    95→    .join("\n");
    96→}
    97→
    98→function formatJS(entries: ColorEntry[], space: ColorSpace): string {
    99→  const colors: Record<string, string> = {};
   100→  for (const e of entries) {
   101→    colors[String(e.lightness)] = colorValue(e, space);
   102→  }
   103→  return `export const grey = ${JSON.stringify(colors, null, 2)} as const;`;
   104→}
   105→
   106→function formatJSON(entries: ColorEntry[], space: ColorSpace): string {
   107→  const tokens: Record<string, { $value: string; $type: string }> = {};
   108→  for (const e of entries) {
   109→    tokens[String(e.lightness)] = {
   110→      $value: colorValue(e, space),
   111→      $type: "color",
   112→    };
   113→  }
   114→  return JSON.stringify({ grey: tokens }, null, 2);
   115→}
   116→
   117→function formatGPL(entries: ColorEntry[]): string {
   118→  const lines = ["GIMP Palette", "Name: Grey Neutrals", `Columns: ${entries.length}`, "#"];
   119→  for (const e of entries) {
   120→    const c = chroma(e.hex);
   121→    const [r, g, b] = c.rgb();
   122→    lines.push(`${String(Math.round(r)).padStart(3)} ${String(Math.round(g)).padStart(3)} ${String(Math.round(b)).padStart(3)}\t${e.label}`);
   123→  }
   124→  return lines.join("\n");
   125→}
   126→
   127→function formatText(entries: ColorEntry[], space: ColorSpace): string {
   128→  const maxLabel = Math.max(...entries.map((e) => e.label.length));
   129→  return entries
   130→    .map((e) => `${e.label.padEnd(maxLabel)}  ${colorValue(e, space)}`)
   131→    .join("\n");
   132→}
   133→
   134→const FORMAT_EXTENSIONS: Record<ExportFormat, string> = {
   135→  css: "css",
   136→  tw4: "css",
   137→  tw3: "js",
   138→  scss: "scss",
   139→  js: "ts",
   140→  json: "json",
   141→  gpl: "gpl",
   142→  text: "txt",
   143→};
   144→
   145→const FORMAT_MIME_TYPES: Record<ExportFormat, string> = {
   146→  css: "text/css",
   147→  tw4: "text/css",
   148→  tw3: "text/javascript",
   149→  scss: "text/css",
   150→  js: "text/javascript",
   151→  json: "application/json",
   152→  gpl: "text/plain",
   153→  text: "text/plain",
   154→};
   155→
   156→export function downloadPalette(
   157→  entries: ColorEntry[],
   158→  format: ExportFormat,
   159→  space: ColorSpace,
   160→): void {
   161→  const content = formatPalette(entries, format, space);
   162→  const ext = FORMAT_EXTENSIONS[format];
   163→  const mime = FORMAT_MIME_TYPES[format];
   164→  const blob = new Blob([content], { type: `${mime};charset=utf-8` });
   165→  const url = URL.createObjectURL(blob);
   166→  const a = document.createElement("a");
   167→  a.href = url;
   168→  a.download = `palette.${ext}`;
   169→  a.click();
   170→  URL.revokeObjectURL(url);
   171→}
   172→
```
</details></tool-use>

     2→export const H = 400;
     3→export const PAD = { top: 24, right: 24, bottom: 36, left: 48 };
     4→export const PLOT_W = W - PAD.left - PAD.right;
     5→export const PLOT_H = H - PAD.top - PAD.bottom;

     8→  return PAD.left + (lightness / 100) * PLOT_W;

I now have a complete picture of the codebase. Here is a thorough critique of the migration guide against the actual implementation.

---

## Migration Guide Critique: HSL to OKLCH

### Summary of the Current Implementation

Before diving into gaps, it's worth establishing what the code actually is, because the guide's "Current State" section is partially wrong or incomplete in ways that matter.

- `generateNeutrals` in `palette.ts` iterates from `lMin` to `lMax` in integer steps (0–100), producing one `ColorEntry` per integer lightness value — up to 101 entries
- `usePalette.ts` feeds those raw entries to `PaletteGrid`, which then samples down to a display count (8, 9, 10, 12, 16, 20, 24, 48, or "all")
- The charts (`SaturationCurve`, `EffectiveSaturation`) receive the full entry array but independently re-call `getSaturation()` themselves to draw the continuous curve — they do not purely consume `ColorEntry` data
- `ColorEntry` stores `lightness` as an integer 0–100, `hsl` as a float tuple, `hslString`, and `hex`. There is no `oklch` field.
- The store holds `lMin`/`lMax` as integers 0–100 (not 0–1 floats)
- `saturation` is stored as a 0–1 float in the store
- `sMod` is stored as a raw internal value (25–∞ range) and the `AdjustmentSlider` converts to/from a percentage display (0 to -100%) via `pctToSMod`/`sModToPct`
- Export already supports `oklch` as a color space output option (via chroma.js), but generates it by converting from hex, not from internal OKLCH values
- `About.tsx` contains its own independent HSL color math, entirely separate from the palette generation pipeline

---

### 1. Gaps and Ambiguities

**The guide does not address the `satMode` parameter at all.**

The store has `satMode: "endpoint" | "target"` and it threads through `PaletteParams`, `generateNeutrals`, and `getSaturation`. Both chart components also consume it. The guide's `chromaCorrection()` signature has no equivalent of `satMode`. The "target" mode normalizes the multiplier so the vertex equals 1.0 (the slider controls mid-lightness chroma, not endpoint chroma). This behavioral duality needs to be explicitly designed for in OKLCH. The guide is completely silent on it.

**The guide's `generatePalette` signature and iteration model don't match the real one.**

The guide's pseudocode iterates `steps` times using float division. The actual code iterates over every integer from `lMin` to `lMax` inclusive — the grid then samples from that full set. Post-migration, the guide implies the palette loop is step-based, but the real architecture generates a dense array and samples downstream. These are compatible but the guide doesn't acknowledge it. It also means `steps` as a parameter doesn't belong at the `generateNeutrals` level — that decision belongs in `PaletteGrid`.

**The "Saturation" slider range and internal mapping are underspecified.**

The guide says to "map the Saturation slider (0–100%) to OKLCH chroma range (0–~0.4 for sRGB, scaled proportionally)." But the slider currently stores a 0–1 float, not 0–100%, and chroma.js's OKLCH range tops out around 0.322 for typical sRGB hues, not 0.4 (0.4 is achievable only for some narrow hue ranges). The guide doesn't specify whether the mapping is linear, what the practical maximum should be, or how `satMode: "target"` interacts with this remapping. This needs a concrete decision before coding begins.

**The `lMin`/`lMax` unit mismatch is not flagged.**

The store holds `lMin` and `lMax` as integers 0–100 (HSL convention). The guide says "map existing Range slider to OKLCH lightness (0–1)." But the guide's own `generatePalette` pseudocode takes `rangeMin`/`rangeMax` as 0–1 floats, and internally divides by 100 (`const L100 = L * 100`). The actual codebase currently works the opposite way — `palette.ts` takes `lMin`/`lMax` as 0–100 integers and divides by 100 at the loop level. The guide would require either a store schema change or a conversion boundary. Neither is called out as a decision point.

**The guide proposes `p=0` disables the curve but doesn't address the default value change.**

The current store default is `sMod: 70`, which corresponds to a visible curve. The guide says "make curve default to off (p=0)." But it doesn't specify whether this means changing the store default or adding a boolean toggle. The `AdjustmentSlider` uses a derived percentage (0 to -100%) where 0% already means p=0. So setting the default to 0% (p=0) is a store default change — but this is a behavioral regression from the user's current experience unless it's deliberate. Worth flagging explicitly.

**The guide doesn't address the `label` field on `ColorEntry`.**

Currently labels are `"grey-{lightness}"` using the HSL integer lightness (e.g., `grey-14`, `grey-50`). Post-migration, OKLCH lightness is a float 0–1. The label needs a new convention. This affects export output (Tailwind, CSS vars, JSON token keys, SCSS vars, JS object keys), the GPL format, and swatch aria/title attributes. The guide is silent on this.

---

### 2. Conflicts with the Guide's Assumptions

**The guide assumes charts re-derive everything from `generatePalette` output. They don't.**

Both `SaturationCurve.tsx` and `EffectiveSaturation.tsx` call `getSaturation()` directly to render the continuous curve (they sample every integer lightness 0–100 independently of the palette entries). Post-migration, these charts need to call `chromaCorrection()` directly to rebuild their curve sampling loops. The guide's checklist item "Saturation Adjustment chart stays — same visualization" glosses over this: the chart's internal curve-drawing code needs its own migration, including receiving the `hue` parameter (which `getSaturation` currently doesn't need, but `chromaCorrection` does).

`SaturationCurve` already receives `hue` from the store, so that's wired, but `EffectiveSaturation` does not read `hue` at all — it only reads `sMod`, `saturation`, and `satMode`. Post-migration, it will need `hue` to compute the hue-dependent center shift. This is a concrete wiring change the guide doesn't mention.

**The guide assumes `chroma-js` can simply be removed or is optional. In reality it is deeply used.**

`chroma-js` is used in:
- `palette.ts` — `chroma.hsl(...)` for all color generation
- `colors.ts` — `chroma.contrast()` for swatch text contrast
- `export.ts` — `chroma(entry.hex)` to convert to other formats (including the existing `oklch` export option)
- `About.tsx` — `chroma.hsl()` throughout the educational strips and `ColorPickerPlane`

The guide mentions "or add `culori` dependency" as an aside. But the migration isn't a choice between custom functions and culori — it's a question of whether chroma-js stays for the remaining non-generation uses (contrast, export conversion, About). The guide treats chroma-js as something you swap out entirely, which isn't accurate. You'd likely keep it for `colors.ts` and `export.ts` while replacing its use in `palette.ts`.

**The guide assumes `ColorEntry` only needs an `oklch` field added at the output stage.**

The guide says "internal palette state should be stored as OKLCH floats." But the `ColorEntry` type is consumed by 7+ components including charts, export, swatches, strip, and About. The `lightness` field (integer 0–100) is used as an SVG x-axis coordinate in both chart components via `toSvgX(e.lightness)`, which assumes the 0–100 scale. If `lightness` becomes a 0–1 OKLCH float, `chartConstants.ts`'s `toSvgX` breaks. Either the field needs to stay scaled 0–100 (converting internally), or `toSvgX` needs to change. The guide doesn't address this.

**The guide's `chromaCorrection()` formula doesn't match `getSaturation()` in "target" mode.**

`getSaturation` has two modes. In "endpoint" mode it matches the guide's formula (returns 1 at extremes). In "target" mode it normalizes so the vertex is 1.0. The guide's `chromaCorrection()` has no equivalent of this normalization. If `satMode: "target"` is kept, someone has to decide what it means in the OKLCH context and implement it. If it's being dropped, that's a UX regression that should be a deliberate decision.

---

### 3. Dependencies and Ordering

The guide's checklist is unordered. Based on the actual code, the dependencies are:

**Phase 1 — Foundation (no UI or behavioral change, can be tested independently):**
1. Define the conversion functions (or decide on culori). Must happen before anything else.
2. Add `maxChromaInSrgb()` and `chromaCorrection()` to a new or updated `palette.ts`.
3. Add OKLCH fields to `ColorEntry` type, keeping all existing fields for now. This is the type contract that unblocks all downstream changes.

**Phase 2 — Core generation (changes the palette output):**
4. Refactor `generateNeutrals` to work in OKLCH internally, populating the new `oklch` field on each entry.
5. Decide and implement the saturation slider → chroma mapping. This requires a decision on max chroma value.
6. Decide the fate of `satMode`. Either port it to OKLCH semantics or remove it (and update `SaturationSlider`, store, `PaletteParams`, chart deps).
7. Change the `sMod` default to 0 (or add a boolean toggle for curve-off default).

**Phase 3 — Chart updates (blocked by Phase 2):**
8. Update `SaturationCurve` to call `chromaCorrection()` instead of `getSaturation()` for curve drawing.
9. Wire `hue` into `EffectiveSaturation` and update its curve sampling to use `chromaCorrection()`.
10. Decide the x-axis scale question: keep `lightness` as 0–100 integer in `ColorEntry` (for chart/export compat) or change `toSvgX` and all callers.

**Phase 4 — Display and export (blocked by Phase 3):**
11. Update `ColorSwatch` copy-on-click and tooltip to offer OKLCH or format choice.
12. Update export's `oklch` color space output to use `entry.oklch` directly instead of converting from hex.
13. Update `label` convention for OKLCH-derived keys.

**Phase 5 — About dialog (isolated, can happen last):**
14. Update `About.tsx` — this is a completely self-contained HSL-based module. Its educational strips, `ColorPickerPlane`, `buildStrips`, and `CurveGraph` all use `chroma.hsl()` and `getSaturation()` directly. The guide says "update About dialog if they reference HSL internals" but this is a significant standalone rewrite, not a minor update. The About dialog effectively contains a second implementation of the core concept.

---

### 4. Missing Concerns

**`About.tsx` is a second, independent implementation that the guide underestimates.**

The educational content in About shows the HSL problem using HSL strips and an HSL color picker plane. The entire point of the About dialog is to demonstrate why HSL is broken. Post-migration, the About dialog either needs to be updated to show OKLCH benefits (a content and code rewrite), or it becomes misleading — it will show an HSL picker plane and an HSL-based parabolic curve to explain a tool that no longer uses HSL internally. The guide's single checklist item "Update About dialog if they reference HSL internals" is grossly insufficient for what is actually there.

**The `SaturationCurve` "Compare" section generates unadjusted colors using `chroma.hsl()` directly.**

Line 68 of `SaturationCurve.tsx`: `chroma.hsl(hue, saturation, e.lightness / 100).hex()`. This "Unadjusted" comparison strip will need to generate flat-chroma OKLCH colors instead. The guide doesn't mention this.

**Export's existing `oklch` color space option currently converts from hex via chroma.js, losing precision.**

`export.ts` already has `case "oklch"` — it converts `entry.hex` back to OKLCH using chroma.js. After migration, `entry.oklch` will have the original OKLCH values without roundtrip loss. The checklist mentions "update swatch labels" but doesn't mention fixing the export pipeline to use the native OKLCH values directly. This is an easy win that should be explicit.

**The `LightnessRange` slider track gradient is hardcoded as an HSL greyscale.**

The track background is `linear-gradient(to right, hsl(0 0% 0%), hsl(0 0% 100%))`. This is a cosmetic concern but worth noting — post-migration, this could theoretically show an OKLCH-accurate lightness ramp, though it's a very minor issue.

**The `FormatSelector` component was not in the provided file list — its behavior around the `space` state is relevant but unverifiable.** This is a minor gap in the analysis request, not the migration guide.

**No mention of the `chroma-js` `oklch()` method precision differences.**

The export already calls `c.oklch()` from chroma.js. After migration, the internal values come from a different implementation (either custom functions or culori). Ensuring the two OKLCH representations are numerically close is not addressed. A brief sanity check step should be explicit in the checklist.

**Performance of `maxChromaInSrgb()` binary search called per swatch per render.**

The binary search runs 12+ iterations per call. At 101 palette entries, that's 1200+ iterations per render of `generateNeutrals`. The charts call `getSaturation` in a 0–100 loop (101 calls); post-migration they'll call `chromaCorrection`, which is fine, but `maxChromaInSrgb` should be pre-computed per (L, H) pair or memoized. The guide doesn't flag this at all.

---

### 5. Scope Clarity of Checklist Items

Going through each item in the migration checklist:

**"Add OKLCH conversion functions or add culori"** — Underspecified. Needs a decision: keep chroma.js for non-generation uses and add custom functions for generation, or replace everything with culori. Either path has different implications for `colors.ts`, `export.ts`, and `About.tsx`.

**"Add `maxChromaInSrgb()` gamut boundary function"** — Clearly scoped, actionable.

**"Refactor palette generation to work in OKLCH internally"** — Monolithic. This is actually items 4–7 above (generation rewrite, satMode decision, default change, saturation mapping). Needs to be broken into at least 3 subtasks.

**"Map existing Saturation slider to OKLCH chroma range"** — Underspecified. Max chroma value is not decided. Linear mapping assumption is not validated. Interaction with satMode is unresolved.

**"Map existing Range slider to OKLCH lightness (0–1)"** — Ambiguous about whether the store changes to floats or a conversion boundary is inserted. Has cascading effects on chart x-axis coordinates and display labels.

**"Wire Adjustment slider to `chromaCorrection()`"** — The `AdjustmentSlider` stores `sMod` as a raw value derived from `pctToSMod(pct) = 2500 / -pct`. The guide's `chromaCorrection()` takes `p` directly. The mapping between `sMod` and `p` needs to be explicitly defined. The current `sMod` range (25–∞ for -100% to -1%) doesn't map cleanly to the guide's `p` parameter. This is underspecified.

**"Add hue-dependent center shift"** — Well-scoped. The `peakLightnessForHue` function is self-contained and `getSaturation` already doesn't use hue so this is additive.

**"Make curve default to off (p=0)"** — Clear outcome, but needs a decision on whether this is a store default change or a new boolean toggle. Behavioral regression if existing users have saved state (not applicable here since there's no persistence layer, but worth noting).

**"Keep all existing UI labels unchanged"** — Clear, low-effort, already done by default.

**"Update swatch labels to show hex + OKLCH or add format toggle"** — The "not a blocker" framing is fine, but `ColorSwatch` currently hardcodes `hsl(${entry.hslString})` in both the button `onClick` (what gets copied to clipboard) and the tooltip. This needs a change even at the "ship with HSL" baseline, because the `hslString` will need to be derived from the OKLCH-internal computation.

**"Update Desmos visualization link / About dialog"** — Severely undersells the About.tsx rewrite. See above.

**"Verify palette quality"** — Not a task, it's a validation step. Fine as a checklist item but not independently actionable — it needs to specify test hues and what "no regression" means.

---

### Files Essential to Understanding the Migration Scope

- `/Users/grant/code/chroma-curve/app/src/lib/palette.ts` — the core that changes
- `/Users/grant/code/chroma-curve/app/src/types/palette.ts` — `ColorEntry` type is the central contract; its shape change propagates everywhere

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-6 2026-03-20 12:42:28Z)**_

Great analysis against the actual codebase. Here's what surfaced:

---

## Clarifying Questions Before Breaking Into Tasks

### 1. `satMode` ("endpoint" vs "target") — not addressed at all
The guide's `chromaCorrection()` only covers "endpoint" mode (returns 1 at extremes). The codebase also has "target" mode where the vertex normalizes to 1.0 — meaning the slider controls mid-lightness chroma, not endpoint chroma. **Is `satMode` being carried forward to OKLCH, redesigned, or dropped?**

### 2. Saturation → Chroma mapping is underspecified
The guide says "map 0–100% to 0–~0.4" but:
- Max sRGB chroma varies by hue (e.g., ~0.322 for most hues, only ~0.4 for narrow ranges)
- Should the mapping be **linear**, or **hue-aware** (slider at 100% = max gamut chroma for that hue)?
- How does `satMode: "target"` interact with this?

### 3. `sMod` ↔ `p` parameter mapping
The current `AdjustmentSlider` converts percentage to `sMod` via `pctToSMod(pct) = 2500 / -pct` (range 25–∞). The guide's `chromaCorrection()` takes `p` directly. **Is `p` the same as `sMod`, or does it need a new mapping?**

### 4. Default curve off = behavioral regression?
Current default is `sMod: 70` (visible curve). Guide says default to `p=0` (curve off). **Is this intentional — new users see flat chroma as the baseline, and the curve becomes opt-in?** Or should the curve still default to on with the new hue-dependent center?

### 5. `About.tsx` is a substantial rewrite, not a one-liner
The About dialog contains an independent HSL implementation: educational strips, a `ColorPickerPlane`, `CurveGraph`, all using `chroma.hsl()` and `getSaturation()` directly. Its entire purpose is demonstrating *why HSL is broken*. Post-migration, do you want to:
- **Rewrite it** to showcase OKLCH benefits?
- **Keep it as-is** (HSL demo stays educational, tool internals change)?
- **Defer entirely** and update later?

### 6. Label convention for OKLCH lightness
`ColorEntry.label` is currently `"grey-{lightness}"` using integer 0–100 (e.g., `grey-14`). These flow into export as Tailwind class names, CSS var names, JSON keys, SCSS vars, etc. OKLCH lightness is 0–1 float. **What label convention post-migration?** Options: scale to 0–100 integer, use 0–1000, keep the HSL-derived integer as the label key...

### 7. `chroma-js` — keep, replace, or supplement?
The guide frames it as "custom functions OR culori." But chroma-js is used in 4 files beyond palette generation (`colors.ts` for contrast, `export.ts` for format conversion, `About.tsx`, `SaturationCurve.tsx` comparison strip). **Keep chroma-js for non-generation uses and add OKLCH math for generation? Or replace wholesale with culori?**

### 8. Performance of `maxChromaInSrgb()` binary search
101 palette entries × 12+ binary search iterations = ~1200 iterations per render. Plus charts re-derive independently. **Should this be memoized or pre-computed per hue?**

---

## Readiness Assessment

**Not quite ready for EPIC/TASK breakdown yet.** Questions 1–4 are design decisions that change the task structure. Specifically:

- **Q1 (satMode)** determines whether there's a "port satMode to OKLCH" task or a "remove satMode" task
- **Q2 (chroma mapping)** determines complexity of the slider refactor
- **Q4 (default behavior)** determines whether there's a migration/UX task
- **Q5 (About dialog)** is potentially its own EPIC or gets deferred entirely

Once those are answered, I see roughly **3 EPICs**: core math migration, chart/display updates, and About dialog rewrite — with ~12-15 tasks total.

_**User (2026-03-20 13:10:51Z)**_

# Answers to Migration Clarifying Questions

## Q1: satMode — carry forward to OKLCH

Keep both modes. They serve different design workflows:

- **"endpoint" mode:** User sets saturation % → that value applies at L=0 and L=100 → curve dips down from there in the midtones. Use case: "I want my darkest/lightest swatches to have exactly this much color, tame the midtones for me."
- **"target" mode:** User sets saturation % → that value applies at the vertex (center) → curve goes up from there toward the edges. Use case: "I have a specific brand color at a specific lightness — keep that color consistent and shape the rest of the palette around it."

Target mode is the more common designer workflow: start from a brand blue (or whatever), lock it in, generate the full scale above and below it. Endpoint mode is for when the designer cares more about the extremes.

Both modes port cleanly to OKLCH — the correction multiplier math is the same, just which point is normalized to 1.0 changes. The slider % maps to OKLCH chroma instead of HSL saturation, but the endpoint-vs-vertex logic is independent of color space.

## Q2: Saturation → Chroma mapping should be hue-aware

Slider at 100% = `maxChromaInSrgb(L, hue)` for the current hue, not a fixed 0.4. Otherwise yellow at 100% looks way more saturated than blue at 100%, which is the exact perceptual inconsistency we're trying to fix.

Compute `maxChromaInSrgb()` at a reference lightness (e.g., the hue's peak lightness from the `PEAK_L_BY_HUE` table) and use that as the 100% ceiling. Linear interpolation from 0 to that max is fine.

For satMode interaction: in "endpoint" mode, 100% = max gamut chroma at the endpoint lightness values. In "target" mode, 100% = max gamut chroma at the vertex lightness. Same principle either way.

## Q3: sMod ↔ p parameter mapping

`p` in the migration guide IS `sMod`. The guide just used a simpler variable name for clarity. Keep the existing `pctToSMod(pct) = 2500 / -pct` conversion as-is. No new mapping needed — the formula accepts the same parameter, it just operates on OKLCH chroma instead of HSL saturation now.

## Q4: Default curve behavior — curve ON by default

This is a greenfield product with no existing users. There is no backwards compatibility concern. That said, the curve should still **default to on** (with the new hue-dependent center shift) because:

- The curve provides artistic value even in OKLCH (subtle midtone refinement)
- The hue-dependent center shift is a new feature that makes the curve smarter — worth showcasing as the default experience
- Users can turn it off (p=0) if they want flat chroma

Use a sensible default `sMod` value that gives a visible but subtle correction in OKLCH. This will need visual tuning since the old default (sMod=70) was calibrated for HSL's much larger distortion — in OKLCH the same value may over-correct. Start with the old default and adjust based on visual review.

## Q5: About dialog — de-prioritize but keep accurate

De-prioritize the About/explainer dialog rewrite. It is not a migration blocker. However, it IS critical that when it is updated, it accurately explains the OKLCH-based approach so users understand what the tool is doing. Create a separate task for this, tagged as post-migration polish. Do not update it as part of the core migration — it's better to leave it temporarily stale than to rush an inaccurate rewrite.

## Q6: Label convention — scale to 0–100 integer

Scale OKLCH lightness (0–1) to 0–100 integer for labels, same convention as today. `grey-14` stays `grey-14`. These labels flow into Tailwind class names, CSS var names, JSON keys, and SCSS vars — they're design token names, not technical values. Do not leak float precision into them.

## Q7: chroma-js — keep for now, do not replace

Keep chroma-js for non-generation uses (contrast checking in colors.ts, format conversion in export.ts, About.tsx, SaturationCurve.tsx comparison strip). Add the OKLCH conversion functions from the migration guide for palette generation only. A wholesale switch to `culori` is a future cleanup task, not a migration blocker. De-prioritize it.

## Q8: Performance — memoize per hue

Precompute `maxChromaInSrgb()` as a lookup array when the hue changes: 101 values (L=0 to L=100 in integer steps), computed once per hue change. Cache and reuse on every render. Hue slider changes are infrequent relative to saturation/adjustment slider changes, so this eliminates the binary search from the hot path. Charts should reference the same cached array, not re-derive independently.

---

## Summary: Ready for task breakdown

All design decisions are resolved. Key structural points:

- **satMode:** both modes carry forward, operating on OKLCH chroma
- **Chroma mapping:** hue-aware, 100% = max gamut chroma for current hue
- **sMod/p:** same parameter, same conversion, no changes
- **Default:** curve on with hue-dependent center, default sMod may need visual re-tuning for OKLCH
- **About dialog:** separate deferred task
- **Labels:** 0–100 integer, unchanged convention
- **chroma-js:** keep, supplement with OKLCH functions
- **Performance:** memoize gamut boundary per hue
- **No backwards compatibility concerns** — greenfield product, no public users

---

     1→# Board System Specification
     2→
     3→> **Status:** Active
     4→
     5→A documentation and project management system designed for human-AI collaboration. Plain text files, minimal process, maximum clarity.
     6→
     7→---
     8→
     9→## Guiding Principles
    10→
    11→These axioms inform every design decision in the system.
    12→
    13→### 1. Never Rely on Discipline
    14→
    15→Human willpower, memory, and routine are points of failure. They will fail — it's a matter of when, not if. The system must be designed so that:
    16→- Nothing falls through the cracks due to forgotten reviews
    17→- No periodic "check on X" rituals are required
    18→- Relevant context surfaces automatically when needed
    19→
    20→### 2. Explicit Over Implicit
    21→
    22→Every outcome must be resolved explicitly. No vague items lingering in documents.
    23→- ADRs must list spawned items or state "no follow-up needed"
    24→- TASKs are actionable or they're not TASKs yet
    25→- If something is worth tracking, decide what type it is
    26→
    27→### 3. Write-Once Artifacts
    28→
    29→Documents should be written once and not require ongoing maintenance.
    30→- ADRs are immutable historical records
    31→- Status reflects validity, not workflow
    32→- No "update this when X happens" requirements
    33→
    34→### 4. One Home for Each Concern
    35→
    36→Every type of information has exactly one place:
    37→- Work to do → TASK
    38→- Decisions made → ADR
    39→- Domain knowledge → REFE
    40→- Unrefined thoughts → NOTE
    41→- External material → RESO
    42→
    43→No overlap. No ambiguity about where something belongs.
    44→
    45→### 5. Binary Actionability
    46→
    47→Something is either actionable or it's not. No intermediate states.
    48→- If you can write the first concrete step → TASK
    49→- If it's still fuzzy → NOTE (refine until it's a TASK, or discard)
    50→- No "maybe someday" purgatory
    51→
    52→### 6. Simple Over Complex
    53→
    54→Resist the pull toward JIRA/PM complexity hell.
    55→- Minimal status values (3 for TASKs, 3 for ADRs)
    56→- No custom fields proliferation
    57→- If a workflow feels heavy, question whether it's necessary
    58→
    59→### 7. The Artifact is Separate from the Work
    60→
    61→- TASK = the work to be done
    62→- ADR = the artifact produced by decision work
    63→- REFE = the artifact produced by knowledge extraction
    64→- Don't conflate process with output
    65→
    66→## Design Goals
    67→
    68→1. **Single source of truth** — metadata lives with the document, no separate index to maintain
    69→2. **Self-identifying files** — type and ID visible in filename
    70→3. **Standalone files** — each file makes sense without the organizational system
    71→4. **Query-friendly** — structured metadata enables filtering and search
    72→5. **Portable** — plain text (markdown + YAML) works anywhere
    73→
    74→---
    75→
    76→## Document Types
    77→
    78→### Overview
    79→
    80→| Type | Purpose | Status Values |
```
</details></tool-use>

<tool-use data-tool-type="read" data-tool-name="Read"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Read**</summary>
 `/Users/grant/Documents/Projects/BoardPM/plugin/spec/board-system-spec.md`

```text
    80→| Type | Purpose | Status Values |
    81→|------|---------|---------------|
    82→| TASK | Actionable work item | `backlog` → `current` → `done` |
    83→| EPIC | Container for related tasks | `planning` → `current` → `done` |
    84→| ADR | Immutable decision record | `active` \| `superseded` \| `deprecated` |
    85→| REFE | Evergreen knowledge base | *(none)* |
    86→| RESO | External material to process | `inbox` → `processing` → `processed` |
    87→| NOTE | Unrefined thoughts (inbox) | `inbox` → `processed` |
    88→
    89→### TASK (Actionable Work)
    90→
    91→A TASK is a single, completable unit of work. If it can't be done in one focused effort, split it into multiple TASKs under an EPIC.
    92→
    93→**Scope check:** If a request involves multiple independently-completable steps, distinct deliverables, or work spanning different code areas, it is likely an EPIC with child TASKs. Quick test: "Could someone meaningfully complete and ship part of this while the rest remains undone?" If yes → EPIC.
    94→
    95→**Required metadata:**
    96→- `type: task`
    97→- `status`: `backlog` | `current` | `done`
    98→- `created`: date
    99→
   100→**Optional metadata:**
   101→- `priority`: `1` (high) | `2` (medium) | `3` (low)
   102→- `due`: target completion date
   103→- `blocked_by`: list of blocking tasks
   104→- `parent`: link to parent epic/task
   105→- `tags`: list of cross-cutting tags
   106→
   107→#### Task Dependencies (blocked_by)
   108→
   109→The `blocked_by` field documents task dependencies. It is **informational**, not a system-enforced constraint.
   110→
   111→**Key points:**
   112→- Status remains workflow-only (`backlog`/`current`/`done`) — no separate "blocked" status
   113→- A task with `blocked_by` stays in `backlog` until blockers are `done`
   114→- When choosing what to work on, tasks with unresolved blockers are skipped
   115→
   116→**Determining if a task is actionable:**
   117→1. Status is `backlog` (not already current or done)
   118→2. Either `blocked_by` is empty/missing, OR all tasks in `blocked_by` have `status: done`
   119→
   120→#### Derived vs Explicit State
   121→
   122→The "is actionable" state is **derived**, not explicit:
   123→
   124→| Approach | How it works | Trade-off |
   125→|----------|--------------|-----------|
   126→| **Derived** (our approach) | Computed on-demand from `blocked_by` + blocker statuses | Requires computation, but always accurate |
   127→| **Explicit** (avoided) | Stored as field like `actionable: true` | Requires updating when blockers change |
   128→
   129→**Why derived is better:**
   130→- **No sync discipline** — Nothing to update when blockers complete
   131→- **Single source of truth** — Each task owns only its own `status`
   132→- **Can't get stale** — Computed from current state
   133→
   134→This aligns with Principle #1 (Never Rely on Discipline).
   135→
   136→### EPIC (Initiative Container)
   137→
   138→Epics are containers for related tasks. They track overall progress of a larger initiative.
   139→
   140→**Required metadata:**
   141→- `type: epic`
   142→- `status`: `planning` | `current` | `done`
   143→- `created`: date
   144→
   145→**Optional metadata:**
   146→- `tasks`: list of child task links
   147→- `tags`: list of cross-cutting tags
   148→
   149→**Rule of thumb:** If it can't be completed in a single focused effort, it's probably an epic that should be split into tasks.
   150→
   151→**Signals that something is EPIC-sized:**
   152→- Multiple distinct verbs/actions in the description (e.g., "add auth, set up roles, and integrate SSO")
   153→- Work spans multiple code areas or concerns
   154→- Sub-steps could be completed and shipped independently
   155→- Would naturally produce multiple PRs or commits
   156→- Description uses "and" to join logically separate deliverables
   157→
   158→### ADR (Architecture Decision Record)
   159→
   160→ADRs are **immutable historical records of decisions already made**. They document what was tried, what was decided, and why. ADRs are written *after* a decision is made, not during the decision-making process.
   161→
   162→> **Key principle**: If you need to track "we must decide X", that's a TASK. The ADR is the artifact produced when the TASK completes.
   163→
   164→**Required metadata:**
   165→- `type: adr`
   166→- `status`: `active` | `superseded` | `deprecated`
   167→- `context_date`: when this decision was made
   168→- `created`: date
   169→
   170→**Optional metadata:**
   171→- `superseded_by`: link to ADR that supersedes this one
   172→- `tags`: list of cross-cutting tags
   173→
   174→#### ADR Status (Validity, Not Workflow)
   175→
   176→ADR status reflects whether the decision still applies, not where it is in a process:
   177→
   178→| Status | Meaning |
   179→|--------|---------|
   180→| `active` | This decision still applies to the project |
   181→| `superseded` | Replaced by a newer ADR (link in `superseded_by`) |
   182→| `deprecated` | No longer relevant (context changed, feature removed, etc.) |
   183→
   184→There is no `proposed` or `pending` status. Decision-making work is tracked as TASKs.
   185→
   186→#### ADR Document Structure
   187→
   188→```markdown
   189→# ADR-NNNN: Title (Decision Summary)
   190→
   191→## Context
   192→What problem were we solving? What was the situation that required a decision?
   193→
   194→## Considered Approaches
   195→What options did we evaluate? Include enough detail to avoid re-trying failed approaches.
   196→
   197→### Approach 1: Name
   198→- What it involves
   199→- Pros and cons
```
</details></tool-use>

# EPIC-0006: Migrate palette generation from HSL to OKLCH

Migrate all internal color math from HSL to OKLCH. The parabolic correction curve shifts from a necessity (compensating for HSL's perceptual brokenness) to an opt-in artistic refinement. Gamut-aware chroma clamping replaces hard clipping. Hue-dependent center shift makes the curve smarter.

## Scope

- OKLCH conversion functions (custom, not culori — keep chroma-js for non-generation uses)
- Memoized sRGB gamut boundary lookup (per-hue cache, 101 lightness steps)
- Hue-dependent chroma correction with both endpoint and target satMode
- Hue-aware saturation→chroma slider mapping (100% = max gamut chroma for current hue)
- Refactored `generateNeutrals` producing OKLCH internally, deriving HSL/hex at output
- Labels stay `grey-{0–100}` (OKLCH lightness scaled to integer)
- Visual tuning of default sMod for OKLCH context

## Key decisions

- **satMode** carries forward (both endpoint and target)
- **chroma-js** stays for contrast, export conversion, About — OKLCH math is additive
- **sMod/p** parameter mapping unchanged (`pctToSMod` stays as-is)
- **Curve defaults to on** — hue-dependent center shift is the new default experience
- **About dialog** is a separate deferred effort (EPIC-0008)

## Reference

- NOTE-0006: Full migration guide with conversion functions, formulas, and checklist
- REFE-0001: Parabolic saturation curve background

```

# EPIC-0007: Update charts and display for OKLCH

Migrate downstream UI that consumes palette data or independently calls `getSaturation()`. Charts need to switch to `chromaCorrection()`, EffectiveSaturation needs hue wired in, swatches and export need OKLCH-native output.

- SaturationCurve: replace `getSaturation()` with `chromaCorrection()`, update unadjusted comparison strip
- EffectiveSaturation: wire `hue` from store, replace `getSaturation()` sampling
- ColorSwatch: update copy-on-click and tooltip for OKLCH/hex output
- Export: use `entry.oklch` directly instead of hex→OKLCH roundtrip

Create `app/src/lib/oklch.ts` with the zero-dependency conversion pipeline from NOTE-0006:

# TASK-0021: Add memoized gamut boundary lookup

Implement `maxChromaInSrgb(L, h)` binary search and a per-hue memoization layer:

- Precompute 101 values (L=0 to L=100 integer steps) when hue changes
- Cache and reuse across renders — hue slider changes are infrequent relative to saturation/adjustment changes
- Charts should reference the same cached array, not re-derive independently
- Binary search precision: `0.0001` chroma tolerance

```

# TASK-0022: Add hue-dependent chroma correction

Implement the correction curve operating on OKLCH chroma:

- `PEAK_L_BY_HUE` lookup table (13 entries, linearly interpolated)
- `peakLightnessForHue(h)` — returns lightness (0–100) where hue reaches peak sRGB chroma
- `chromaCorrection(L, hue, p)` — parabolic multiplier with hue-dependent center point
- Support both satMode behaviors:
  - **endpoint**: multiplier = 1.0 at L=0 and L=100, dips at center (existing behavior ported)
  - **target**: multiplier normalized so vertex = 1.0 (slider controls center chroma)

```

# TASK-0024: Refactor generateNeutrals to OKLCH internals

Rewrite `generateNeutrals` in `app/src/lib/palette.ts` to:

1. Iterate L from `lMin` to `lMax` (integer 0–100, same as today)
2. Convert L to OKLCH lightness (L/100)
3. Apply gamut-aware chroma clamping via memoized `maxChromaInSrgb` cache
4. Apply optional parabolic curve via `chromaCorrection()` (when sMod !== 0)
5. Derive RGB/hex/HSL at output boundary using OKLCH conversion functions
6. Populate `ColorEntry.oklch` with native OKLCH values
7. Keep `ColorEntry.lightness` as integer 0–100, `label` as `grey-{lightness}`

The `getSaturation()` function can be kept temporarily for chart compatibility (migrated in EPIC-0007) or removed if charts are updated simultaneously.

# TASK-0025: Implement hue-aware saturation-to-chroma mapping

Map the existing Saturation slider (0–1 float in store) to OKLCH chroma:

- 100% = `maxChromaInSrgb(L_ref, hue)` where `L_ref` is the hue's peak lightness from `PEAK_L_BY_HUE`
- Linear interpolation from 0 to that max
- satMode interaction:
  - **endpoint mode**: reference lightness = endpoint lightness values (lMin/lMax)
  - **target mode**: reference lightness = vertex lightness (peak for hue)
- Store value stays 0–1 float — the mapping to absolute chroma happens in `generateNeutrals`

```

# TASK-0026: Tune default sMod for OKLCH

Visual review of default `sMod=70` in OKLCH context. The old default was calibrated for HSL's large perceptual distortion — in OKLCH the same value may over-correct since the baseline is already perceptually even.

- Test across multiple hues (red, yellow, blue, green) at various saturation levels
- Compare with curve off (sMod→∞ / pct=0%) to gauge correction magnitude
- Adjust default to give a visible but subtle refinement
- Document the chosen value and rationale

## Scope Assessment

When the user requests a **TASK**, evaluate whether it is actually EPIC-sized before creating it. Look for these signals:

- Multiple distinct steps or deliverables in the description
- Multiple action verbs joined by "and" (e.g., "add auth and set up roles and integrate SSO")
- Work that spans different code areas or concerns
- Sub-steps that could be completed and shipped independently

# EPIC-0009: Migrate from chroma.js to culori

Replace chroma.js with [culori](https://github.com/Evercoder/culori) as the sole color library. After EPIC-0006 lands, chroma.js remains used in four areas: contrast checking (`colors.ts`), export format conversion (`export.ts`), About dialog (`About.tsx`), and the SaturationCurve comparison strip. Culori has first-class OKLCH support, better gamut mapping, and a smaller bundle (tree-shakeable).

## Scope

- Replace `chroma.contrast()` in `colors.ts` with culori's contrast utilities
- Replace `chroma(hex).css('hsl')` / `.oklch()` / etc. in `export.ts` with culori format conversions
- Replace `chroma.hsl()` calls in `About.tsx` (educational strips, ColorPickerPlane) with culori
- Replace `chroma.hsl()` in SaturationCurve comparison strip with culori
- Evaluate whether the custom OKLCH functions from TASK-0020 can be replaced by culori equivalents (or kept for zero-dependency core)
- Remove chroma.js dependency from `package.json`

## Blocked by

- EPIC-0006 (core OKLCH migration must land first — chroma.js is still load-bearing until then)
- EPIC-0008 (About dialog rewrite — avoid migrating chroma.js calls that will be rewritten anyway)

## Reference

- culori repo: https://github.com/Evercoder/culori
- Q7 answer in NOTE-0006 analysis: "A wholesale switch to culori is a future cleanup task, not a migration blocker"

```

---
type: reference
created: 2026-03-20

# Color Name API

REST API for resolving color values to human-readable names. Given a hex/RGB value, returns the closest named color from a curated dataset of 30,000+ color names.

## Links

- GitHub: https://github.com/meodai/color-name-api
- Live API: https://api.color.pizza

## Relevance to Chroma Curve

Could be used to display descriptive color names on palette swatches (e.g., "Slate Blue", "Dusty Rose") alongside hex/HSL/OKLCH values. Useful for designers who think in color names rather than numeric values. Would be an optional, network-dependent feature — palette generation stays local.

## API Shape

- `GET /v1/?values=hex1,hex2,...` — returns closest named colors for a list of hex values
- Supports batch requests (send all swatch hex values at once)
- Returns: name, exact hex match, closest named hex, distance (ΔE)

```
